mixed.question: Which two famous writers died on the same day — November 22, 1963 — overshadowed by JFK's assassination?
mixed.fact: Both literary giants' deaths went nearly unreported because Kennedy was assassinated the same day.

mixed.question: True or False: Mark Twain was born and died in years when Halley's Comet was visible from Earth.
options: Only his birth coincided with the comet
Only his death coincided with the comet
mixed.fact: Twain predicted his own death would coincide with the comet's 1910 return — and he was right.

mixed.question: King Umberto I of Italy reportedly met a restaurant owner who was his exact double in appearance, birthday, and marriage date. How did both men die?
options: Both drowned on the same day
Both were assassinated on the same day
Both died of heart attacks a week apart
Both perished in the same earthquake
mixed.fact: The restaurant owner was shot the same day King Umberto I was assassinated in Monza in 1900.

mixed.question: In a strange coincidence, the Titanic novel 'Futility' described a huge ship called 'Titan' sinking after hitting an iceberg. How many years before the real disaster was it written?
mixed.fact: Morgan Robertson's 1898 novella eerily predicted the Titanic's size, speed, and insufficient lifeboats.

mixed.question: Which country has the most time zones of any nation on Earth, spanning 12 different offsets?
mixed.fact: France's overseas territories give it 12 time zones — more than Russia's 11 contiguous zones.

mixed.question: Which African country is home to Erta Ale, one of the world's only persistent lava lakes, located in the Danakil Depression?
mixed.fact: The Danakil Depression drops to 125 meters below sea level and is one of Earth's hottest places.

mixed.question: In 2025, which Caribbean island nation officially designated a large new marine protected area exceeding 100,000 square kilometers?
mixed.fact: The Bahamas expanded ocean protections significantly, covering deep-sea ecosystems and coral reefs.

mixed.question: What surprising property did scientists discover about water in 2024, involving a second liquid-liquid phase transition at extreme supercooling?
options: Water splits into two distinct liquid phases of different densities
Water conducts electricity like a metal
mixed.fact: Supercooled water can separate into high-density and low-density liquid forms — confirming decades of theory.

mixed.question: Which element, long considered too unstable to study, was shown in recent experiments to form a stable compound with nitrogen under extreme pressure?
mixed.fact: Promethium's bonding chemistry was finally characterized in 2024 at Oak Ridge National Laboratory.

mixed.question: How many times more synapses does the human brain have compared to stars in the Milky Way galaxy?
mixed.fact: The brain has ~100 trillion synapses versus ~200 billion stars — roughly a 500x difference.

science.question: What is the only number that is exactly twice the sum of its digits?
science.fact: 18 = 2 × (1+8). No other positive integer shares this elegant self-referential property!

science.question: How many trees can be formed from a labeled set of 6 vertices, according to Cayley's formula?
science.fact: Cayley's formula gives n^(n-2), so 6^4 = 1296 labeled trees — a surprisingly large number for just 6 points.

science.question: True or False: There exists a prime number whose digits, when reversed, form a different prime — and whose digit sum is also prime. 13 is one example.
options: True, but only for two-digit primes
science.fact: These are called 'emirps.' 13 reversed is 31, and 1+3=4... actually 37→73 with digit sum 10 works differently — many emirps exist!

science.question: In early 2026, mathematicians announced progress on the Hadwiger-Nelson problem. What does this problem ask for?
options: The chromatic number of the plane
The largest prime gap under a trillion
The minimum clique cover of a hypergraph
The packing density of irregular polygons
science.fact: This asks how many colors are needed so no two same-colored points are exactly 1 unit apart — still unsolved after 75 years!

science.question: Which insect genus provides evidence that evolution can 'reverse' lost structures, contradicting Dollo's Law?
science.fact: Stick insects re-evolved wings after millions of wingless years, shocking biologists who believed complex traits couldn't return.

science.question: What surprising adaptation have some lizard populations on Greek islands evolved in under 40 years, documented since the 1970s?
science.fact: Italian wall lizards on Pod Mrčaru evolved cecal valves — a brand-new gut structure — in roughly 30 generations!

science.question: Which term describes the evolutionary process where two unrelated species each become more like the other, benefiting both?
science.fact: In Müllerian mimicry, multiple toxic species evolve to resemble each other, sharing the cost of predator education.

science.question: In quantum mechanics, what does the 'no-cloning theorem' state is impossible?
options: Creating an exact copy of an unknown quantum state
science.fact: This 1982 theorem is fundamental to quantum cryptography — eavesdroppers literally cannot copy quantum information undetected.

science.question: Which counterintuitive quantum effect allows particles to pass through energy barriers they classically shouldn't be able to cross?
science.fact: Without quantum tunneling, the Sun wouldn't shine — protons in its core lack the energy to fuse classically!

science.question: In 2025, researchers achieved a major milestone by demonstrating quantum error correction below the 'threshold theorem' requirement using which company's processor?
science.fact: Google's Willow chip showed that adding more qubits actually reduced errors — a first for quantum computing history.

history.question: During the French Revolution, what unusual unit of time replaced the traditional week under the Republican Calendar?
history.fact: Workers hated the décade system because they got only 1 rest day in 10 instead of 1 in 7.

history.question: Which French Revolutionary figure was nicknamed 'The Sea-Green Incorruptible'?
history.fact: Thomas Carlyle coined the nickname, referencing Robespierre's pale complexion and rigid moral stance.

history.question: True or False: During the Reign of Terror, the Revolutionary government briefly attempted to replace Christianity with a 'Cult of the Supreme Being.'
options: It was called the Cult of Reason only
It was proposed but never officially enacted
history.fact: Robespierre personally organized a massive festival for the Cult of the Supreme Being in June 1794.

history.question: In 2025, historians at the Archives nationales revealed that which surprising item was found preserved in a Revolutionary-era time capsule from 1795?
options: A lock of Marie Antoinette's hair
A draft constitution written by Condorcet
history.fact: The preserved bread samples helped scientists analyze the severe grain shortages that fueled revolutionary anger.

history.question: Which of Henry VIII's wives was queen for the shortest period of time?
history.fact: Anne of Cleves was queen for only six months but outlived all of Henry's other wives.

history.question: Who was the mysterious 'Princes in the Tower' suspect that a 2013 archaeological dig in Leicester famously helped re-evaluate?
history.fact: Richard III's skeleton was found under a Leicester car park, revealing severe scoliosis confirming Tudor-era accounts.

history.question: Which Tudor-era law made it technically treasonous to predict the death of the monarch?
history.fact: The 1542 amendment was partly aimed at astrologers and prophets who might inspire plots against Henry VIII.

history.question: Which Industrial Revolution invention dramatically increased cotton thread production by a factor of eight over the spinning jenny?
history.fact: Richard Arkwright's water frame produced thread strong enough for warp, revolutionizing factory-based textile production.

history.question: How many children under age 10 were estimated to be working in British coal mines before the Mines and Collieries Act of 1842?
history.fact: Some children as young as five worked as 'trappers,' sitting alone in darkness opening ventilation doors.

history.question: Which city, often overlooked in favor of Manchester, was actually Britain's largest manufacturing town by population in the 1811 census?
history.fact: Manchester narrowly edged out Birmingham in 1811 but was not incorporated as a borough until 1838.

popculture.question: Which 1980s pop star was originally fired from his first band, The Executives, before going on to massive solo success with 'Never Gonna Give You Up'?
popculture.fact: Rick Astley was a tea boy at a recording studio before Pete Waterman discovered him at age 19.

popculture.question: In the 1980s, which toy company produced the infamous 'Teddy Ruxpin' talking bear that could also secretly play regular music cassettes?
popculture.fact: Worlds of Wonder also made the Nintendo Entertainment System's light gun — they went bankrupt by 1990.

popculture.question: True or False: The iconic 1980s film 'Ferris Bueller's Day Off' was shot in just 69 days, one of the fastest major studio productions of that decade.
options: False — it was completed in only 49 days
popculture.fact: John Hughes wrote the screenplay in under a week, one of his fastest-ever scripts.

popculture.question: Which 1985 arcade game, featuring a time-travel motorcycle, was the first laserdisc game to use entirely computer-generated imagery?
popculture.fact: Time Traveler by Sega used a concave mirror to project holographic-looking 3D images above the cabinet.

popculture.question: What was the original name of Twitter when Jack Dorsey first conceived of the platform in 2005?
popculture.fact: Dorsey's original concept was called 'Status' — inspired by AOL Instant Messenger's away messages.

popculture.question: Which social media platform was the first to reach 1 billion monthly active users?
popculture.fact: Facebook crossed 1 billion monthly users in October 2012, just eight years after launching from a dorm room.

popculture.question: In 2025, which platform surpassed 2 billion monthly active users, shocking analysts who had predicted its decline?
popculture.fact: Despite facing bans and regulations worldwide, TikTok's user growth accelerated through short-form video dominance.

popculture.question: Who accidentally revealed the gender of their baby on live television in 2025, causing the clip to go viral with over 200 million views?
popculture.fact: Tom Holland's flustered reaction became one of the most-shared celebrity moments of 2025.

popculture.question: Which celebrity famously bought a high school yearbook photo of themselves at auction in 2019 to prevent its release, only for the image to leak anyway?
popculture.fact: The 'Streisand Effect' applies here — Lady Gaga's attempt to suppress it made the photo far more famous.

popculture.question: How many outfit changes did Beyoncé make during her record-breaking 2023 Renaissance World Tour?
popculture.fact: Beyoncé's custom outfits included pieces from over 20 designers, with some costing six figures each.

geography.question: Which European country has the highest number of UNESCO World Heritage Sites per capita?
geography.fact: Tiny Malta has three UNESCO World Heritage Sites for under 520,000 people — an extraordinary cultural density.

geography.question: True or False: Liechtenstein is one of only two doubly landlocked countries in the world.
options: It is not landlocked at all
geography.fact: Only Liechtenstein and Uzbekistan are doubly landlocked — surrounded entirely by other landlocked countries.

geography.question: In 2025, which European country officially opened the Fehmarnbelt tunnel link, the world's longest immersed road-rail tunnel?
geography.fact: The Fehmarnbelt tunnel connects Denmark to Germany beneath the Baltic Sea, spanning about 18 kilometers.

geography.question: Which European capital city was built across 14 islands connected by 57 bridges?
geography.fact: Stockholm spreads across 14 islands where Lake Mälaren meets the Baltic Sea — earning it the 'Venice of the North' nickname.

geography.question: Which South American country contains the world's largest salt flat, the Salar de Uyuni?
geography.fact: Salar de Uyuni spans over 10,000 square kilometers and holds roughly 50–70% of the world's lithium reserves.

geography.question: What is the only South American country where Dutch is an official language?
geography.fact: Suriname gained independence from the Netherlands in 1975, making it the smallest country in South America.

geography.question: Which South American river system has a tributary (the Casiquiare canal) that naturally connects it to the Amazon basin?
geography.fact: The Casiquiare is the world's largest natural canal, bizarrely linking two major river basins — a geographic anomaly.

geography.question: Which island nation is the only country in the world that spans all four hemispheres (Northern, Southern, Eastern, Western)?
geography.fact: Kiribati's islands straddle the equator and the International Date Line, placing it in all four hemispheres simultaneously.

geography.question: How many inhabited islands make up the Republic of the Maldives?
geography.fact: The Maldives has roughly 1,190 total islands, but only about 200 are inhabited — the rest are used for tourism or industry.

geography.question: Which island nation's highest point, Mount Apo, rises to 2,954 meters despite the country being entirely composed of islands?
geography.fact: Mount Apo on Mindanao is an active stratovolcano home to the critically endangered Philippine eagle.

sports.question: Which country hosted the FIFA World Cup despite never having qualified for the tournament before being automatic hosts?
sports.fact: Qatar 2022 became the first host nation to lose their opening World Cup match ever.

sports.question: How many FIFA World Cup finals have been decided by a penalty shootout?
sports.fact: The 1994, 2006, and 2022 finals all went to penalties — Argentina won the most recent one.

sports.question: Who is the only player to have scored in every round of a single FIFA World Cup, from group stage through the final?
sports.fact: Jairzinho scored in all six matches of Brazil's 1970 campaign — a feat never repeated since.

sports.question: True or False: The 2026 FIFA World Cup will be the first to feature 48 teams in the tournament.
sports.fact: The expansion from 32 to 48 teams makes the 2026 tournament co-hosted by USA, Canada, and Mexico.

sports.question: Which golfer holds the record for the lowest single-round score (63) in a major championship the most times?
sports.fact: Branden Grace shot a 62 at the 2017 Open Championship — the first ever sub-63 round in a major.

sports.question: Who was the oldest golfer to win a major championship, capturing the 1968 PGA Championship at age 48?
sports.fact: Julius Boros beat Arnold Palmer and Bob Charles in a playoff to claim the title at 48 years old.

sports.question: At the 2025 Masters Tournament, which golfer claimed the green jacket at Augusta National?
sports.fact: Scottie Scheffler won his second Masters, cementing his dominance in the mid-2020s golf landscape.

sports.question: Which Formula 1 circuit is the shortest on the current calendar at just 3.337 km per lap?
sports.fact: Despite being the shortest track, the Monaco GP is one of the longest races by time at ~2 hours.

sports.question: Who was the youngest Formula 1 World Drivers' Champion before Max Verstappen claimed the record in 2015?
sports.fact: Vettel won his first title at 23 years and 134 days in 2010 — Verstappen broke it at age 24.

sports.question: In the 2025 Formula 1 season, which team returned to the grid after acquiring another team's entry?
sports.fact: Audi took over the Sauber team entry, marking the German manufacturer's first F1 factory effort.

food.question: Which spice gets its deep red color from the compound crocin and requires about 75,000 flowers to produce a single pound?
food.fact: Saffron stigmas are hand-picked at dawn before flowers fully open, making it the world's most expensive spice.

food.question: True or False: Black pepper and white pepper come from the same plant, just harvested at different stages of ripeness.
food.fact: White pepper is simply black pepper with the outer fruit layer removed after soaking ripe berries.

food.question: In 2025, researchers discovered that which common herb contains compounds that can cross the blood-brain barrier and may improve memory consolidation?
food.fact: Rosemary's compound 1,8-cineole was found at measurable levels in brain tissue after dietary consumption.

food.question: Which spice, native to Guatemala, was used as currency by the ancient Maya and has seeds that taste like a mix of cinnamon, nutmeg, and cloves?
food.fact: Allspice trees are dioecious — you need both male and female trees to produce the prized berries.

food.question: What unexpected ingredient makes traditional Worcestershire sauce non-vegetarian?
food.fact: The original Lea & Perrins recipe ferments anchovies for 18 months to develop its umami flavor.

food.question: Which plant-based protein source is considered 'complete' with all nine essential amino acids, despite being a seed rather than a grain?
food.fact: Quinoa, hemp hearts, and chia seeds are all complete proteins — rare among plant foods.

food.question: Jackfruit is increasingly used as a meat substitute. How heavy can a single jackfruit grow?
food.fact: Jackfruit is the largest tree-borne fruit in the world, sometimes requiring two people to carry one.

food.question: Which Italian pasta shape's name literally translates to 'little ears'?
food.fact: Orecchiette originated in Puglia and is traditionally shaped by pressing dough with a butter knife.

food.question: In authentic Italian cuisine, which ingredient is NEVER used in a traditional carbonara sauce?
food.fact: Adding cream to carbonara is considered a culinary sin in Rome — the silkiness comes solely from eggs and cheese.

food.question: The high-end Italian white truffle (Tuber magnatum) is primarily found in which region and has never been successfully cultivated commercially?
food.fact: Alba's white truffles can sell for over $4,000 per pound because all attempts at farming them have failed.

music.question: Which one-hit wonder band behind 'Come On Eileen' (1982) took their name from a character in a 1950s Italian film?
music.fact: The name 'Dexys' referenced Dexedrine, while leader Kevin Rowland was inspired by Italian neorealist cinema.

music.question: True or False: Chumbawamba, known for 'Tubthumping,' were actually an anarchist punk collective active for over 30 years.
options: They were a manufactured pop group
music.fact: Chumbawamba formed in 1982 as radical anarcho-punks and didn't disband until 2012—a 30-year career for a 'one-hit wonder.'

music.question: Chesney Hawkes' 'I Am the One and Only' (1991) was written by which legendary songwriter who also fronted The Who?
music.fact: Nik Kershaw, himself nearly a one-hit wonder in the US with 'Wouldn't It Be Good,' penned the iconic track.

music.question: Which 2025 viral one-hit sensation topped global Spotify charts for weeks before the artist seemingly vanished from public view?
options: Artemas with 'I Like the Way You Kiss Me'
music.fact: Artemas reached #1 in multiple countries but gave almost zero interviews, fueling one-hit wonder speculation.

music.question: Which K-Pop group's member Woozi has personally produced over 100 songs for the group, an unusual feat in idol music?
music.fact: SEVENTEEN's self-producing model is rare in K-Pop, with Woozi credited on the vast majority of their discography.

music.question: Japanese city pop singer Mariya Takeuchi's 'Plastic Love' went massively viral decades after its 1984 release. How many views has the YouTube video surpassed?
music.fact: A random YouTube upload in 2017 turned this obscure 1984 B-side into one of the most iconic retrowave anthems.

music.question: Which K-Pop soloist became the first Asian solo artist to headline Coachella's main stage in 2025?
music.fact: LISA's headline slot marked a historic milestone for both K-Pop and Southeast Asian representation at Coachella.

music.question: Which pioneering electronic duo released 'Homework' in 1997 and famously never showed their faces without helmets after 1999?
music.fact: Daft Punk adopted robot personas partly to shift focus from celebrity to music—they retired in 2021.

music.question: What BPM range defines the electronic genre 'drum and bass,' making it one of the fastest mainstream dance genres?
music.fact: Drum and bass typically runs 160–180 BPM, yet dancers often move at half-time, giving it a deceptively relaxed feel.

music.question: Which electronic producer created the 'Windowlicker' EP and is known for releasing music under multiple cryptic aliases including AFX and Polygon Window?
music.fact: Richard D. James (Aphex Twin) has released music under at least 12 different aliases throughout his career.

technology.question: Which tech billionaire reportedly lived in a small apartment above a garage while already worth over $1 billion?
technology.fact: Zuckerberg rented a modest home even after Facebook's IPO, famously driving a basic Volkswagen GTI.

technology.question: True or False: Tech billionaire Michael Dell started his computer company from a dorm room at the University of Texas at Austin.
options: He started it from his parents' garage
He started it after graduating from Stanford
technology.fact: Dell launched PCs Limited in 1984 from Room 2713 of the Dobie Center dorm with just $1,000.

technology.question: In 2025, which tech billionaire briefly surpassed $400 billion in personal net worth, a historic first?
technology.fact: Musk's wealth surged past $400 billion in late 2024/early 2025, largely driven by SpaceX valuations.

technology.question: Which tech billionaire was a professional poker player before co-founding a major software company?
technology.fact: Larry Ellison partly funded the creation of Oracle with winnings and savings from poker and odd jobs.

technology.question: What was the original name of Microsoft's AI assistant Cortana during its internal development?
technology.fact: Microsoft codenamed the project after Halo's AI character from the start — the name was never changed.

technology.question: Which programming language did Bill Gates and Paul Allen write for the Altair 8800, launching Microsoft?
technology.fact: Gates and Allen wrote Altair BASIC without ever having access to the actual hardware — they used an emulator.

technology.question: How many billions of dollars did Bill Gates's foundation pledge to spend annually by 2026, as announced in 2024?
technology.fact: The Gates Foundation announced plans to increase annual spending to $9 billion, up 50% from previous levels.

technology.question: NASA's OSIRIS-REx mission returned samples from asteroid Bennu. What unexpected finding surprised scientists about the sample?
options: It contained liquid water molecules trapped in crystals
It had traces of artificial polymers
technology.fact: Bennu samples contained water molecules locked inside phosphate crystals, hinting at a watery parent asteroid.

technology.question: Which NASA spacecraft holds the record for the fastest human-made object ever, exceeding 400,000 mph?
technology.fact: Parker Solar Probe uses repeated Venus gravity assists to dive closer to the Sun, accelerating to extreme speeds.

technology.question: What is the name of NASA's rotorcraft that achieved the first powered flight on another planet?
technology.fact: Ingenuity was designed for just 5 flights but completed 72 before its rotor blade was damaged in 2024.

nature.question: Which great ape has been observed using medicinal plants to treat wounds, as documented in a 2024 study of a Sumatran population?
nature.fact: A Sumatran orangutan was filmed applying a chewed medicinal plant directly to a facial wound, healing it completely.

nature.question: True or False: Gorillas can catch human colds and other respiratory illnesses.
nature.fact: Gorillas are so genetically close to humans that even a common cold virus can be lethal to them.

nature.question: How many species of great apes (excluding humans) are currently recognized by primatologists?
nature.fact: The seven are: Bornean orangutan, Sumatran orangutan, Tapanuli orangutan, eastern gorilla, western gorilla, chimpanzee, and bonobo.

nature.question: Which primate is the only known non-human species to consistently ask questions of humans using sign language?
options: None — no non-human primate has ever spontaneously asked questions
nature.fact: Despite learning hundreds of signs, no ape has reliably demonstrated asking questions — a key cognitive difference from human children.

nature.question: Which reptile can survive being frozen solid during winter, with up to 65% of its body water turning to ice?
nature.fact: Wood frogs produce glucose as a cryoprotectant — their hearts stop beating and restart when they thaw in spring.

nature.question: What surprising ability does the tuatara of New Zealand possess that distinguishes it from all other living reptiles?
options: It can regenerate its entire tail including the spine
It has a functional third 'parietal' eye
It can breathe underwater using its skin
It produces venom from glands in its jaw
nature.fact: The tuatara's third eye has a lens and retina but gets covered by scales in adulthood — it likely senses light changes.

nature.question: Which venomous amphibian uses bony spines on its ribs to puncture through its own skin and inject toxins?
nature.fact: The Spanish ribbed newt literally pushes its sharp ribs through toxic skin glands as a defense — and heals rapidly afterward.

nature.question: In tropical rainforests, approximately what percentage of all sunlight is blocked before reaching the forest floor?
nature.fact: The dense canopy and understory absorb nearly all light, making the forest floor one of the darkest habitats on land.

nature.question: Which rainforest tree is known for having enormous buttress roots that can extend over 30 feet from the trunk?
nature.fact: Kapok trees can reach 200 feet tall — their buttress roots act like flying buttresses on cathedrals, stabilizing shallow root systems.

nature.question: What counterintuitive fact is true about the soil in most tropical rainforests?
options: It is among the most nutrient-rich soil on Earth
It is extremely thin and nutrient-poor
It contains unusually high levels of nitrogen
It is naturally alkaline due to limestone bedrock
nature.fact: Most nutrients in rainforests are locked in living biomass, not soil — fallen matter decomposes and is reabsorbed almost immediately.

film.question: Which film holds the record for winning the most Oscars without winning Best Picture?
film.fact: Cabaret won 8 Oscars in 1973 but lost Best Picture to The Godfather — the biggest such gap ever.

film.question: Who became the oldest person ever to win an acting Oscar, winning Best Supporting Actor at age 82?
film.fact: Christopher Plummer won for Beginners (2012), beating the previous record held by Jessica Tandy.

film.question: At the 2025 Academy Awards, which film won Best International Feature Film?
options: The Seed of the Sacred Fig
film.fact: I'm Still Here was Brazil's first-ever Best International Feature win, directed by Walter Salles.

film.question: True or False: An animated film has been nominated for the Best Picture Oscar.
options: True — Both Beauty and the Beast and Up were nominated
False — no animated film has ever been nominated
film.fact: Both Beauty and the Beast (1991) and Up (2009) received Best Picture nominations, plus Toy Story 3.

film.question: Which martial arts star performed his own stunt falling from a clock tower in 'Project A,' inspiring Jackie Chan's reputation?
film.fact: Jackie Chan's clock tower fall was a deliberate homage to Harold Lloyd and nearly broke his neck.

film.question: In the 'John Wick' franchise, what is the name of the secret hotel that serves as a sanctuary for assassins?
film.fact: The Continental's 'no business on hotel grounds' rule became one of modern action cinema's most iconic concepts.

film.question: Which action star was working as a school bus driver in New York before landing a breakout role in 'The Terminator'?
film.fact: Schwarzenegger was actually already famous from bodybuilding — it was Lance Henriksen who was originally cast as the Terminator.

film.question: Which 1977 arthouse film by David Lynch, shot over five years on a shoestring budget, became a midnight movie phenomenon?
film.fact: Lynch partly funded Eraserhead with a paper route he delivered at 2 AM during production.

film.question: Which Iranian director was sentenced to prison and banned from filmmaking, yet secretly made 'Taxi' (2015) using a dashboard camera?
film.fact: Panahi smuggled his film 'This Is Not a Film' out of Iran on a USB drive hidden inside a cake.

film.question: Which 2024 independent film, shot largely on an iPhone, earned its director a Best Director nomination at the 2025 Oscars?
film.fact: Parts of The Brutalist used AI-generated voice work, sparking major industry debate about AI in independent film.

politics.question: Which small EU member state held the rotating presidency of the Council of the European Union in the first half of 2025?
politics.fact: Poland held the EU Council presidency from January to June 2025, its first time in the role since 2011.

politics.question: True or False: The European Parliament has more seats than the U.S. House of Representatives and the U.S. Senate combined.
options: It depends on the legislative term
politics.fact: The European Parliament has 720 seats, while the U.S. Congress has only 535 combined.

politics.question: Which EU treaty first introduced the concept of European citizenship, granting citizens the right to live and work in any member state?
politics.fact: The 1992 Maastricht Treaty created EU citizenship — a concept many Europeans didn't realize existed for years.

politics.question: Which European country's far-right Freedom Party (FPÖ) won the most seats in its 2024 parliamentary election, a first since World War II?
politics.fact: The FPÖ, founded in 1956 partly by former Nazis, became Austria's largest parliamentary party for the first time.

politics.question: In his 1963 'Ich bin ein Berliner' speech, JFK's pronunciation technically translates to what in some German dialects?
politics.fact: In some dialects, 'ein Berliner' means a jelly doughnut, though Berliners themselves understood Kennedy perfectly.

politics.question: Who delivered the famous 'Rivers of Blood' speech in 1968, warning against immigration to Britain?
politics.fact: Powell was immediately fired from the Shadow Cabinet, but the speech made him enormously popular with the public.

politics.question: Which world leader's 1960 UN General Assembly appearance is famous for the shoe-banging incident, though photographic proof remains disputed?
politics.fact: No authenticated photograph of Khrushchev actually banging his shoe exists — the incident may be partly mythologized.

politics.question: What was the codename of the CIA's secret program to build tunnels under Berlin to tap Soviet communication lines in the 1950s?
politics.fact: The Soviets knew about the tunnel from the start — their mole George Blake had betrayed the plan before construction began.

politics.question: Which neutral European country secretly allowed the CIA to use its territory for Cold War spy flights, a fact only declassified in the 2000s?
politics.fact: Officially neutral Sweden secretly cooperated extensively with NATO and the CIA throughout the entire Cold War.

politics.question: How many Soviet submarines were involved in the 1962 Cuban Missile Crisis naval quarantine confrontation, each carrying nuclear torpedoes?
politics.fact: On one submarine, officer Vasili Arkhipov single-handedly vetoed a nuclear torpedo launch, possibly preventing World War III.
